2018 · AAAI 2018 · Post-training & RL

Rainbow: Combining Improvements in Deep Reinforcement Learning

Six independently-published fixes to DQN — Double Q-learning, prioritized replay, dueling networks, multi-step returns, distributional RL, and Noisy Nets — turn out to be largely complementary. Combined into one agent, they reach a 223% median human-normalized score across 57 Atari games, and a systematic ablation study ranks which of the six are actually pulling their weight.

♪ listen — audio episode
3-voice deep dive · mp3 ↗ · rss feed ↗
Matteo Hessel, Joseph Modayil, Hado van Hasselt, Tom Schaul, Georg Ostrovski, Will Dabney, Dan Horgan, Bilal Piot, Mohammad Azar, David Silver · DeepMind
venue AAAI 2018 citations 2,665 topic Post-training & RL source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-07 (Semantic Scholar)
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By late 2017, the "DQN family" had splintered into a pile of separately-published fixes, each addressing a different weakness of the original 2015 DQN algorithm: Double DQN corrects Q-learning's overestimation bias, prioritized experience replay resamples the transitions with the most to learn from, dueling networks split the Q-value into a state-value and an action-advantage stream, multi-step returns (as used in A3C) trade off bias and variance to propagate reward faster, distributional RL (C51) learns the whole return distribution instead of just its mean, and Noisy Nets replace ε-greedy exploration with learned per-layer stochasticity. Each of these had its own paper, its own ablations, and its own claimed gains over vanilla DQN — but almost nothing was known about whether they were complementary or redundant. A few pairwise combinations existed (Prioritized DDQN, Dueling DDQN, Dueling DDQN + prioritized replay), but no one had tried combining all six, and it was entirely plausible that some of them competed for the same source of improvement — e.g., double Q-learning's bias correction and distributional RL's return-clipping could easily interact rather than stack.

The gap is therefore not a new algorithmic idea but a missing systematic study: does stacking six unrelated ideas onto the same base agent actually add up, and if it does, which of the six are doing the real work?

// how it works

The method

Rainbow is an engineering integration, not a new update rule: it takes the DQN loss and rewrites it as a multi-step, double, distributional Bellman update, then wraps every linear layer of the resulting dueling network in a noisy layer, and finally reprioritizes replay by the loss this new target actually minimizes. Concretely, the paper starts from the 1-step categorical (C51) distributional loss and swaps in the n-step return: the target distribution becomes dₜ⁽ⁿ⁾ = (R꜀⁽ⁿ⁾ + γ⁽ⁿ⁾z, pθ(St+n, a*t+n)), where the bootstrap action a*t+n is selected by the online network (double Q-learning) but evaluated with the frozen target network. Because the agent no longer minimizes squared TD-error but a KL divergence between predicted and target return distributions, prioritized replay's sampling probability is redefined to be proportional to that KL loss rather than to |TD-error|, which the authors argue is more robust when returns are stochastic. The network itself is a dueling architecture — a shared convolutional trunk feeding separate value and advantage streams — but reshaped to output a full categorical distribution (51 atoms, support [−10, 10]) per action rather than a single scalar, with the two streams aggregated per-atom before a softmax normalizes each action's distribution. Every linear layer in this network — value stream, advantage stream, and the final projection — is then replaced with a Noisy Net layer, y = (b + Wx) + (bnoisy ⊙ ε_b + (Wnoisy ⊙ ε_w)x), whose weight and bias noise (ε_w, ε_b) is factorised Gaussian noise the network can learn to anneal state-by-state; with Noisy Nets in place, ε-greedy exploration is switched off entirely (ε = 0).

y = (b + Wx) + (bnoisy ⊙ εb + (Wnoisy ⊙ εw)x) // Noisy Net linear layer replacing every y = b + Wx in the dueling network — the deterministic stream plus a learned, self-annealing noise stream

Hyperparameters were tuned per-component starting from each original paper's settings, then adjusted by manual coordinate descent: a lower Adam learning rate of α/4 (vs. DQN's 0.00025, with Adam replacing RMSprop), n = 3 for multi-step returns (n = 5 was competitive early but n = 3 won by 200M frames), priority exponent ω = 0.5, and — enabled by prioritized replay — a shorter 80K-frame warm-up instead of DQN's usual 200K. All hyperparameters are identical across all 57 games; Rainbow is a single fixed configuration, not one retuned per game.

// what they found

The results

Across the standard 57-game Atari 2600 suite, Rainbow's best-snapshot median human-normalized score is 223% under the no-ops evaluation regime and 153% under the human-starts regime — both well clear of every baseline in the comparison (Table 2 below). In wall-clock terms, matching DQN's final performance takes about 7M frames (under 10 hours on a single GPU), and Rainbow overtakes the best final score of every other baseline within 44M frames of a 200M-frame run (≈10 days), a run whose duration varies by less than 20% across all the variants tested.

Agentno-ops medianhuman-starts median
DQN79%68%
DDQN*117%110%
A3C*—116%
Noisy DQN118%102%
Prioritized DDQN*140%128%
Dueling DDQN*151%117%
Distributional DQN164%125%
Rainbow223%153%

* scores taken from the original publication rather than re-run by the Rainbow authors; unmarked rows are the authors' own re-implementations.

The ablation study is the paper's other headline contribution. Removing prioritized replay or multi-step returns each produced the largest drops in median performance — Rainbow beat either single-component ablation on 53 of 57 games — and multi-step removal hurt not just early learning but final performance too. Distributional RL ranked next: the "no distribution" ablation tracked the full agent almost exactly for the first 40M frames, then fell behind, lagging specifically on games where performance was already at or above human level. Noisy Nets helped in aggregate (removing them and reverting to ε-greedy hurt the median) but produced a mix of large per-game drops and occasional per-game gains. Dueling networks and double Q-learning were the two components with the least aggregate effect: removing dueling showed no significant median difference (though large per-game swings in both directions), and double Q-learning's effect was similarly limited in the median — the authors note this may partly be an artifact of clipping the distributional support to [−10, 10], since actual returns often exceed that range and get compressed on both sides, which could be masking the overestimation bias double Q-learning is designed to fix.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

Rainbow is explicitly a "kitchen sink" paper — its entire contribution is combining six pre-existing ideas and measuring what happens — and that framing is exactly where it should be read most carefully. The ablation study is a classic leave-one-out design: remove one component, keep the other five, compare medians. That design measures each component's marginal contribution inside this particular six-way combination, not its contribution in isolation, and the paper's own discussion of double Q-learning is a candid admission of the problem — its effect is confounded with the [−10, 10] distributional support clipping, so "double Q-learning barely matters here" is a statement about this specific combination's hyperparameters, not about double Q-learning generally. With six components there are 2⁶ possible ablation combinations and the paper tests only 7 (full Rainbow + 6 single-removals), so genuine two- and three-way interaction effects — e.g., does prioritized replay matter less once distributional RL is already reweighting attention via its own KL-based priority? — are simply invisible to this design.

The baseline comparisons in Table 2 are also a mix of provenance: DDQN, Prioritized DDQN, Dueling DDQN, and A3C's numbers are taken directly from their original papers (marked with an asterisk), run on different codebases, hardware, and possibly different preprocessing details years apart, while DQN, Noisy DQN, and Distributional DQN are the Rainbow authors' own re-implementations run under one shared setup. That's precisely the kind of cross-codebase, cross-team comparison this graph's own Henderson et al., "Deep Reinforcement Learning that Matters" shows can move continuous-control results by as much as the algorithmic idea itself — random seed, codebase, and even network width. Henderson's audit was on continuous-control policy-gradient methods (TRPO/DDPG/PPO/ACKTR), a different regime from Atari's discrete-action value-based agents, so its exact numbers don't transfer directly — but its structural warning does: none of Table 2's rows report a seed count, a variance estimate, or a confidence interval, so "Rainbow's median beats Dueling DDQN's median by 72 points" is a single run against another team's single run, not a statistically supported claim.

One number the paper doesn't foreground is worth pulling out: Rainbow's own no-ops-minus-human-starts gap is 70 points (223% → 153%) — larger than Distributional DQN's 39-point gap or Dueling DDQN's 34-point gap, and far larger than DQN's 11-point gap. A bigger drop when episodes are seeded from human trajectories rather than the agent's own no-op starts is exactly the standard signature of overfitting to one's own training-time state distribution, and it's proportionally worse for Rainbow than for five of the six components it's built from.

baselines

Mixed provenance — DDQN/Prioritized DDQN/Dueling DDQN/A3C numbers are copied from their original papers (different codebases/hardware), while DQN/Noisy DQN/Distributional DQN are the authors' own re-runs in one shared setup; no seed counts or variance reported for any row.

contamination

Not a data-leakage risk in the supervised sense, but Rainbow's no-ops→human-starts drop (70 points) is proportionally the largest of any agent in Table 2, suggesting more overfitting to its own start-state distribution than the simpler baselines it's compared against.

what's missed

The leave-one-out ablation can't see interaction effects among the six components (only 7 of 2⁶ combinations are tested); the median hides real per-game reversals (Rainbow loses to other agents on bowling, private_eye, skiing); and no compute/wall-clock comparison is given for running all six systems together versus a cheaper subset.

would raise confidence

Multiple seeds with reported variance per curve; a factorial (not leave-one-out) ablation over at least the top components; a compute-normalized comparison against simpler combinations; and a re-run of the double-Q ablation with the distributional support widened, since the authors themselves flag support-clipping as a likely confound.

// bottom line

The headline claim — that prioritized replay and multi-step learning matter most, distributional RL matters next, and dueling/double-Q barely register in aggregate — is a real and useful empirical finding, but it's a single-run, single-codebase, single-hyperparameter-setting finding under Atari's specific reward-clipped, frame-stacked training regime. Treat the component ranking as "true for this combination, on this benchmark, under these settings" rather than a general law of which DQN improvements matter.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Rainbow is an integration paper, not a new algorithm: Double Q-learning, prioritized replay, dueling networks, multi-step returns, distributional RL (C51), and Noisy Nets combine into one agent that reaches 223% median human-normalized score (no-ops) / 153% (human starts) across 57 Atari games, against DQN's 79%/68% — matching DQN's final score by 7M frames and beating every baseline's final score by 44M frames.
  2. The six components are not equally important: prioritized replay and multi-step learning drove the largest gains (beating either single ablation on 53/57 games), distributional RL mattered mainly for pushing already-strong games higher, Noisy Nets helped in aggregate with mixed per-game effects, and dueling networks and double Q-learning barely moved the median at all in this combination — the latter possibly masked by the [−10,10] distributional support clipping, per the authors' own discussion.
  3. The comparison table mixes numbers copied from four different original papers with the authors' own re-implementations, none with reported seeds or variance — a reminder, reinforced by this graph's own Henderson et al. critique of RL benchmarking, to read single-run median comparisons across codebases with real caution, even when the combined result is genuinely strong.
